question: What is the fundamental difference between a CLI program and an event-driven GUI program?
correct: A CLI runs instructions sequentially from top to bottom; a GUI program waits for user events (clicks, key presses) and reacts to them as they occur
incorrect: A CLI program uses the heap; a GUI program uses the stack for all operations
A CLI program can only run on Windows; a GUI program works on all platforms
A CLI runs in a single thread; a GUI runs each component in its own separate thread
feedback: CLI: main() executes line by line. GUI: after setup, the program enters an event loop managed by the Event Dispatch Thread. Actions (button clicks, text entry) trigger registered event handlers (listeners) asynchronously.

question: What is a JFrame in Java Swing?
correct: The top-level window container for a Swing GUI application — provides the title bar, borders, and close buttons
incorrect: A layout manager that arranges components in a frame-like border around the window
A type of panel that displays a border around its contents
An interface that all Swing windows must implement to be displayed
feedback: JFrame is the outer window. Components (JLabel, JButton, JTextField) are added to the JFrame's content pane. Key methods: setTitle(), setSize(), setVisible(true), setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE).

question: What does setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE) do?
correct: Terminates the entire JVM when the user closes the window
incorrect: Hides the window but keeps the JVM running
Asks the user for confirmation before closing
Disposes only the current JFrame, leaving other windows open
feedback: Without EXIT_ON_CLOSE, closing the window hides it but the JVM keeps running. JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE ensures the program ends when the main window is closed. JFrame.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE destroys the frame but keeps the JVM alive.

question: What is FlowLayout in Swing?
correct: A layout manager that positions components left-to-right in a row, wrapping to the next row when the window is too narrow
incorrect: A layout manager that divides the window into five regions (North, South, East, West, Center)
A layout manager that places every component at an absolute x,y coordinate
A layout manager that arranges components in a grid of equal-sized cells
feedback: FlowLayout is Swing's default layout for JPanel. It arranges components like words on a page. When the window is resized, components reflow. Simple but unpredictable for complex UIs.

question: What is BorderLayout in Swing?
correct: A layout manager that divides the window into five regions: North, South, East, West, and Center — the Center region expands to fill remaining space
incorrect: A layout manager that draws a decorative border around the window
A layout manager that places components in a single row along the top border
A layout manager that creates a grid where components can span multiple rows and columns
feedback: BorderLayout is JFrame's default layout. Add components with: frame.add(button, BorderLayout.NORTH). Center expands to fill remaining space; the other four regions take only the space they need. Unused regions are invisible.

question: What is the purpose of the ActionListener interface?
correct: To define the actionPerformed() callback that is called when a user interacts with a component, such as clicking a button
incorrect: To define how the component renders itself on screen
To handle keyboard arrow-key navigation between components
To synchronize GUI updates across multiple threads
feedback: ActionListener has one method: void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e). Register it with button.addActionListener(listener). Since ActionListener is a functional interface, a lambda can replace the anonymous inner class: button.addActionListener(e -> handleClick());

question: What is the difference between JTextField and JTextArea?
correct: JTextField is a single-line text input; JTextArea is a multi-line text input
incorrect: JTextField accepts only numbers; JTextArea accepts any text
JTextField is read-only; JTextArea is editable
JTextField displays HTML; JTextArea displays plain text only
feedback: JTextField: single line, for short input. JTextArea: multiple lines, for longer text. Wrap JTextArea in JScrollPane to add scroll bars: panel.add(new JScrollPane(textArea)).

question: What is the purpose of ButtonGroup in Swing?
correct: To link multiple JRadioButtons so that selecting one automatically deselects the others — enforcing mutual exclusion
incorrect: To place multiple buttons in a horizontal row using a built-in layout
To assign the same ActionListener to a group of JButtons
To visually group buttons inside a bordered panel
feedback: ButtonGroup itself is not a visual component — it is invisible. Add radio buttons to it: group.add(rb1); group.add(rb2); Then add rb1 and rb2 to a panel for display. The group ensures only one can be selected at a time.

question: What is JOptionPane used for?
correct: Displaying simple dialog boxes — messages, input prompts, or confirmation dialogs — without building a full custom window
incorrect: Creating the main application window for a Swing program
Displaying tooltips when the user hovers over a component
Validating form input before processing it
feedback: JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(frame, "Hello!") shows an info popup. JOptionPane.showInputDialog(frame, "Name:") prompts for a string. JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog returns YES/NO/CANCEL. Quick dialogs without building a custom JFrame.

question: What is input validation in the context of a GUI program?
correct: Checking that user-provided text is in the expected format before processing it — typically using try-catch around Integer.parseInt()
incorrect: Preventing users from typing more characters than a JTextField can display
Using BorderLayout to ensure input fields never overlap
Checking that event listeners are registered before a button is clicked
feedback: Users can type anything; your code must handle invalid input. Wrap Integer.parseInt(field.getText()) in a try-catch (NumberFormatException e) and show a JOptionPane error message instead of crashing.

question: What is the Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern?
correct: An architecture pattern separating data logic (Model), visual components (View), and the coordinator between them (Controller)
incorrect: A pattern that uses three JFrames — one for input, one for output, and one for error messages
A Swing-specific pattern where all GUI code goes in main()
A layout manager that arranges panels into Model, View, and Controller regions
feedback: Model: pure Java classes with fields, methods, no GUI imports. View: JFrame, JPanel, components — no business logic. Controller: listens to View events, calls Model methods, updates View. MVC keeps GUI code and logic separate and testable.

question: Why should business logic not go inside ActionListener handlers (or lambda event listeners)?
correct: Mixing GUI and logic violates MVC, making code harder to test, reuse, and maintain
incorrect: ActionListener methods run on a different thread where logic cannot execute safely
Java enforces that ActionListener implementations may only call Swing methods
Business logic makes ActionListener listeners too slow to respond to rapid clicks
feedback: An ActionListener handler should call a controller method: controller.submitForm(). The actual logic lives in the Model/Controller. This allows unit-testing the logic without constructing a GUI.

question: Which claims about JFrame setup are correct?
correct: setVisible(true) must be called last, after all components have been added
setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE) is required to end the JVM when the window closes
setSize(width, height) sets the window size in pixels
incorrect: Components added after setVisible(true) are never displayed
JFrame uses FlowLayout by default
feedback: setVisible(true) last: if called before adding components, the window may appear empty. EXIT_ON_CLOSE: without it, closing the window hides it but keeps the JVM running. JFrame's default layout is BorderLayout (JPanel uses FlowLayout).

question: Which claims about layout managers are correct?
correct: BorderLayout divides the container into five regions: North, South, East, West, Center
The Center region of BorderLayout expands to fill all remaining space
FlowLayout is the default layout manager for JPanel
incorrect: FlowLayout is the default layout manager for JFrame
BorderLayout can only hold one component per region, but it can place any number of regions
feedback: JFrame: BorderLayout (default). JPanel: FlowLayout (default). In BorderLayout, each region holds exactly one component — use nested JPanels to group multiple components per region.

question: Which claims about event listeners in Swing are correct?
correct: ActionListener is a functional interface and can be implemented with a lambda
A single component can have multiple ActionListeners registered to it
The actionPerformed() method receives an ActionEvent that describes the event
incorrect: addActionListener() can only be called once per component
Event listeners run before the user's action is processed by the JVM
feedback: Multiple listeners: Swing calls each in the order they were added. ActionListener as lambda: button.addActionListener(e -> doSomething()). The ActionEvent's getSource() returns the component that fired it.

question: Which claims about the MVC pattern in GUI applications are correct?
correct: The Model should not import any Swing or GUI classes
The View creates and arranges components but contains no business logic
The Controller is the mediator: it listens to the View and updates the Model and View accordingly
incorrect: In a Swing MVC, the Model extends JPanel
MVC requires three separate .java files — one for each layer
feedback: Model: pure data and logic (no GUI). View: all JComponents, layout, listeners registered but logic delegated. Controller: coordinates the two — receives UI events, calls Model methods, pushes results back to View. Files can be organized however is convenient.

question: Which claims about JCheckBox and JRadioButton are correct?
correct: JCheckBox allows independent selections — each checkbox is selected or deselected independently
JRadioButtons in the same ButtonGroup are mutually exclusive — selecting one deselects the others
ButtonGroup is not a visual container; radio buttons must still be added to a JPanel for display
incorrect: ButtonGroup is a subclass of JPanel that arranges radio buttons in a row
JCheckBox and JRadioButton can both be added to a ButtonGroup for mutual exclusion
feedback: JCheckBox: each independent (for multi-select). JRadioButton + ButtonGroup: one selected at a time. ButtonGroup is logical grouping only — no visual appearance. Adding checkboxes to ButtonGroup would make them mutually exclusive, which is non-standard.

question: The Swing top-level window class is ___.
correct: JFrame
feedback: JFrame is the main window. It contains the title bar, borders, and a content pane where you add components. Create with: JFrame frame = new JFrame("My App");

question: The layout manager that divides a container into North, South, East, West, and Center regions is ___.
correct: BorderLayout
feedback: BorderLayout is JFrame's default. Use frame.add(component, BorderLayout.CENTER). The Center region expands to fill remaining space.

question: To handle a button click, you register an ___ with the JButton.
correct: ActionListener
feedback: button.addActionListener(e -> handleClick()) or button.addActionListener(new MyListener()). ActionListener has one method: void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e).

question: To group multiple JRadioButton components so that only one can be selected at a time, you use a ___.
correct: ButtonGroup
feedback: ButtonGroup group = new ButtonGroup(); group.add(rb1); group.add(rb2); ButtonGroup is not visible — also add rb1 and rb2 to a JPanel.

question: To add scroll bars to a JTextArea, wrap it in a ___.
correct: JScrollPane
feedback: panel.add(new JScrollPane(textArea)) adds vertical and horizontal scroll bars when the content exceeds the visible area. JTextArea alone does not scroll.

question: In MVC, the layer that holds data and business logic with no GUI code is called the ___.
correct: Model
feedback: The Model contains fields, methods, and logic. It does not import Swing. The View holds all GUI components. The Controller coordinates between them — processes View events and updates Model and View.

question: Match each Swing component to its purpose.

pairs.definition: The top-level window container for the application
Displays non-editable text or an image
A clickable button that fires ActionEvents

feedback: JFrame: outer window. JLabel: display-only text. JTextField: user input (one line). JButton: action trigger. JTextArea is multi-line input; JCheckBox/JRadioButton are selection inputs.

question: Match each layout manager to its behavior.

pairs.definition: Arranges components left-to-right in rows, wrapping when the window is too narrow
Divides the container into North, South, East, West, and Center regions
Arranges components in equally-sized rows and columns
Requires absolute x,y positioning using setBounds() — not recommended for resizable windows

feedback: FlowLayout: JPanel default. BorderLayout: JFrame default. GridLayout: equal rows/cols. null layout: pixel-exact but brittle — components do not adapt when resized.

question: Match each MVC layer to its responsibility in a Swing application.

pairs.definition: Contains data fields, business rules, and logic — no Swing imports
Creates and arranges all JComponents; displays data from the Model
Registers listeners on View components; calls Model methods; updates the View
The callback interface that the Controller implements to respond to GUI events

feedback: Model: pure Java. View: pure Swing layout, no logic. Controller: mediator — decouples View from Model. ActionListener (or lambda) is the mechanism the Controller uses to react to button clicks.

question: Match each JOptionPane method to what it displays.

pairs.definition: Displays an informational popup with an OK button
Displays a text-entry prompt and returns the user's input String
Asks a yes/no/cancel question and returns the user's choice as an integer
Displays a fully customized dialog with user-defined button labels

feedback: JOptionPane is the quick-dialog toolkit. showMessageDialog: info/error messages. showInputDialog: simple string input. showConfirmDialog: YES_OPTION / NO_OPTION / CANCEL_OPTION. showOptionDialog: full customization.
